symptoms: Dark brown spots with a yellow halo appear first on older leaves.
Concentric ring patterns develop inside the spots.
Leaves yellow and dry from the lower canopy upward.
organic_treatments: Remove and destroy heavily infected lower leaves to reduce spore load.
Spray Trichoderma viride or Trichoderma harzianum suspension at 5 g/L of water, repeat every 7-10 days for 2-3 sprays.
Alternate with Pseudomonas fluorescens at 5 g/L of water after 7 days if disease pressure remains high.
Use neem oil at 3 ml/L of water with a sticker, spray every 7 days during humid weather.
Apply Jeevamrutha as a soil drench around the root zone every 15 days to improve crop vigor.
prevention: Use healthy seedlings and avoid overhead irrigation late in the day.
Maintain adequate plant spacing and airflow.
Mulch to prevent soil splash onto leaves.
Rotate away from tomato and other solanaceous crops for at least one season.
source_doc: Organic vegetable disease management notes and ZBNF plant protection guidance

symptoms: Water-soaked grey-green patches appear rapidly on leaves.
White mold growth is visible on the underside of infected leaves in humid weather.
Fruit develops brown rot and collapses quickly.
organic_treatments: Remove severely infected plant parts immediately and destroy them away from the field.
Spray Trichoderma harzianum or Trichoderma viride at 5 g/L of water every 7 days at first symptom appearance.
Use Pseudomonas fluorescens at 5 g/L of water in alternation with Trichoderma after 5-7 days.
Apply neem oil at 5 ml/L of water once weekly during cool humid weather.
If crop is under severe stress, use a cow urine spray at 3% solution as a general foliar support, avoiding direct spray during hot sunlight.
prevention: Avoid dense canopy and improve ventilation.
Do not leave wet foliage overnight when humidity is high.
Use clean staking and avoid working in the field when plants are wet.
Monitor weather closely because the disease spreads rapidly in cool, wet conditions.
source_doc: Organic tomato blight management guidance and extension disease alerts

symptoms: Diamond-shaped grey spots with a brown border appear on leaves.
The neck of the panicle can rot and dry suddenly.
Severe infection causes poor grain filling and white heads.
organic_treatments: Seed treat with Trichoderma viride at 4 g/kg seed before sowing.
Spray Pseudomonas fluorescens at 5 g/L water at tillering and repeat after 10 days if symptoms appear.
Apply neem leaf extract 5% as a foliar spray once weekly during high disease risk periods.
Use Jeevamrutha soil application to maintain plant vigor and microbial balance every 15 days.
prevention: Use blast-tolerant varieties where available.
Avoid excess nitrogen that makes the crop too lush.
Use clean seed and maintain field sanitation.
Prefer balanced nutrition and timely sowing to reduce disease escape risk.

symptoms: The central shoot dries and can be pulled out easily in the vegetative stage, called dead heart.
During the reproductive stage, the ear head turns white and empty, called white ear.
Small holes may be visible near the stem base with borer damage inside.
organic_treatments: Install pheromone traps at 8-10 traps per acre from early crop stage and replace lures as recommended.
Release or conserve natural enemies and avoid broad-spectrum chemicals.
Spray neem oil at 5 ml/L of water every 7 days when moth activity is high.
Use a cow urine spray at 3% solution as a repellent support spray on the foliage every 7-10 days.
Remove and destroy dead hearts if infestation is localized.
prevention: Synchronize sowing or transplanting in an area to reduce pest buildup.
Avoid excess nitrogen that attracts pests.
Keep bunds and field edges clean of host grasses.
Monitor trap catches weekly and act early.

symptoms: Circular yellowing patches appear in the field and may expand into hopper burn.
Sticky honeydew is visible on leaves and stems.
Black sooty mold develops on the sticky surfaces.
organic_treatments: Avoid excess nitrogen and stop any lush over-fertilization immediately.
Spray neem oil at 5 ml/L of water with a sticker every 5-7 days during infestation.
Use cow urine spray at 3% solution as a contact-repellent spray in the evening.
Conserve natural predators such as spiders and mirid bugs by avoiding disturbance and chemicals.
Where feasible, use yellow sticky traps along field edges to monitor and reduce adults.
prevention: Use resistant varieties when available.
Maintain proper spacing and avoid continuous rice cropping without breaks.
Monitor the lower canopy regularly for early colonies.
Do not over-irrigate and keep the crop balanced with organic inputs.

symptoms: Holes are seen in the bolls and lint may be damaged inside.
Frass or insect droppings are visible near entry points.
Bolls may open prematurely or become rotten.
organic_treatments: Install pheromone traps at 8-10 traps per acre and inspect them twice weekly.
Use neem oil at 3-5 ml/L of water every 7 days on the crop canopy.
Apply botanical sprays such as neem seed kernel extract or neem leaf extract in rotation weekly.
Use hand removal of damaged bolls in small plots and destroy infested material away from the field.
Use light traps at night where appropriate to monitor moth activity.
prevention: Avoid prolonged flowering overlap with nearby host crops.
Keep field borders and weeds managed.
Use timely sowing so the crop escapes peak pest pressure.
Encourage beneficial insects by maintaining habitat diversity.

symptoms: Small circular brown spots appear on leaves.
Spots may merge and the leaf area becomes yellow and dry.
Severe infection causes early defoliation and yield loss.
organic_treatments: Seed treat with Trichoderma viride at 4 g/kg seed before sowing.
Spray Pseudomonas fluorescens at 5 g/L water every 10 days once first spots appear.
Apply neem oil at 3 ml/L of water as a supportive foliar spray every 7 days.
Use Jeevamrutha around the root zone every 15 days to support crop resilience.
prevention: Use disease-free seed and crop rotation.
Avoid dense crop stand and promote airflow.
Remove crop debris after harvest to reduce carryover inoculum.
Monitor the crop from early vegetative stage onward.
source_doc: Organic groundnut foliar disease management notes

symptoms: Lower leaves yellow first and then wilt progressively.
The pseudostem shows internal brown streaks when cut open.
Plants may collapse and stop producing marketable bunches.
organic_treatments: Remove severely affected mats and destroy them away from the plantation.
Drench the pit or basin with Trichoderma harzianum at 10 g/L water around the root zone, repeat after 15 days.
Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens at 5 g/L of water as a root-zone drench monthly.
Use well-decomposed compost and Jeevamrutha in healthy plants to improve root-zone biology.
Avoid moving contaminated soil or suckers from infected fields.
prevention: Use disease-free planting material only.
Do not plant in fields with a known wilt history unless sanitation is strong.
Maintain drainage and avoid water stagnation.
Sterilize tools between plants if working in infected plots.

symptoms: Small water-soaked lesions appear on the leaves.
Lesions become purple with a yellow halo around them.
Leaves dry from the tip and bulb development is reduced.
organic_treatments: Spray Trichoderma viride at 5 g/L of water every 7 days at early symptom stage.
Alternate with Pseudomonas fluorescens at 5 g/L of water after 5-7 days.
Use neem oil at 3 ml/L of water with a spreader once weekly during humid periods.
Avoid waterlogging and improve field aeration to reduce disease spread.
prevention: Use healthy seedlings and avoid crowded planting.
Do not irrigate late in the day when leaf wetness will persist overnight.
Remove crop residues after harvest.
Rotate with non-allium crops.

symptoms: Shoot tips dry first and the dieback moves downward along the branches.
Fruit anthracnose appears as sunken dark lesions on chilli fruits.
Affected fruit shrivels and quality drops quickly.
organic_treatments: Prune and destroy infected shoots and fruits away from the field.
Spray Trichoderma harzianum or Trichoderma viride at 5 g/L of water every 7-10 days.
Use neem oil at 3 ml/L of water on the canopy in alternation with Trichoderma sprays.
Apply cow urine spray at 3% solution as a supportive foliar spray during early infection.
Maintain plant vigor with Jeevamrutha soil application every 15 days.
prevention: Use healthy, disease-free transplants.
Avoid water stress and root injury.
Keep field and nursery sanitation strict.
Ensure good spacing and prompt removal of infected plant parts.
source_doc: Organic chilli fungal disease management guidance
